author: jsign
content:
Yes, the main goal of `public_keys` is to assist the guest program in verifying faster the transaction signatures. 

This is an optimization idea we benchmarked last year, originally used by Risc0 in their Reth guest program fork.

A bit of context and links:
- Applying this optimization idea to Reth https://github.com/paradigmxyz/reth/pull/17841
- Benchmarks I did with that optimization on https://github.com/eth-act/zkevm-benchmark-workload/pull/185 (~20% speedup)

So TL;DR is that is for allowing a decent double digit % speedup in guest programs, based on verifying a signature knowing the public key is faster than doing a ful ecrecover.

author: 0xrubo
content: can i count on always having them for each tx? i presume their indices are the same, right? so if no pub key, it fails the execution, right?

author: jsign
content: Yes, it must be always be a 1:1 match with the tx list. No matter if the tx fails or not. No dedupe, index match exactly always.

author: 0xrubo
content: great. to reiterate -- if no key found, it fails instead of trying to recover address itself

author: jsign
content: By "no key found" you mean the lengths of txs list and pub_keys not matching?

author: 0xrubo
content: yeah. or say pub key is invalid

author: jsign
content: Yes, on both cases is an invalid stateless execution. (i.e. bad guest program input)
